title: Frozen Evaporator Coil
complaint: No cooling. Ice visible on the refrigerant lines at the air handler.
initialData: Ice on suction line at air handler
Supply air barely moving from registers
System has been running for 6+ hours

s1.prompt: Ice on the coil, very low suction pressure, low superheat, barely any airflow. What is the first action?

options.label: Turn the system to fan-only to thaw the coil, then find the root cause
Check refrigerant charge with the coil frozen

s1-wrong.prompt: You cannot accurately diagnose a frozen system. The coil must be thawed first. Adding refrigerant to a frozen system is dangerous.
s1-wrong.feedback: Always thaw a frozen coil before diagnosis. Run fan-only for 30–60 minutes.

s2.prompt: After 45 minutes on fan-only, the coil is thawed. You check the filter — it is completely blocked. Static pressure is 1.1 in. w.c. What do you do?

options.label: Replace the filter and recheck operating pressures

s2-wrong.prompt: A completely blocked filter is the obvious root cause of low airflow. Fix the airflow restriction before checking charge.

s3.prompt: After replacing the filter, static pressure is 0.48 in. w.c. Suction pressure: 118 psig. Superheat: 9°F. Temperature split: 19°F. What do you do?

options.label: Add refrigerant — superheat could be higher
System is within spec — verify and document, advise customer on filter maintenance

s3-wrong.prompt: All parameters are within spec. The root cause was a blocked filter causing low airflow and coil freeze. No refrigerant adjustment needed.

options.label: Verify, document, and advise on filter maintenance

pass.prompt: Correct. Blocked filter → low airflow → coil temperature below 32°F → freeze. Replacing the filter restored normal operation.
pass.feedback: Frozen coil root causes: (1) low airflow — dirty filter, closed registers, failed blower; (2) low refrigerant charge. Always check airflow first.
